subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: In the natural history of disease, point A (exposure) marks the start of which stage?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

front: Point C, the onset of symptoms, begins which stage of disease?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What does point D represent in the natural history of disease?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: List the four stages of the natural history of disease in order.
back: Stage of susceptibility -> stage of subclinical disease -> stage of clinical disease -> stage of recovery, disability or death.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What is 'lead time' in screening?
back: The interval from point D (usual time of diagnosis) back to point B (when screening detects the disease) - i.e. 'D to B'.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What is the bias called when screening appears to increase survival only because the disease was diagnosed earlier, without changing the actual outcome?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What is the first case of a disease in the community called?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What is the first observed (detected) case called?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What is the time interval between a primary case and a secondary case called?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What is the time between entry of the organism and the appearance of symptoms called?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What is the time between entry of the organism and maximum communicability called?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: Isolation is applied to whom?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: Quarantine is applied to whom?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What is the quarantine period (QP) of yellow fever?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What are the three components of the epidemiological triad?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: How does the epidemiological triangle differ from the triad?
back: It adds Time at the centre - agent, host and environment sit at the corners with time in the middle.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: In the advanced triad of epidemiology, what falls under 'Agent'?
back: Causative factors, risk factors, and environmental exposures.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: In the advanced triad of epidemiology, what falls under 'Time'?
back: Time characteristics, incubation/latency, length of disease process, and trends and cycles.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: In the advanced triad of epidemiology, what falls under 'Host'?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: In the advanced triad of epidemiology, what falls under 'Environment'?
back: Place characteristics, and biological, physical and psychosocial environments.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What are the three domains of the Human Development Index (HDI)?
back: Knowledge (mean & expected years of schooling), income (real GNI per capita), and life expectancy at birth.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: How is the 'knowledge' component of HDI measured?
back: Mean and expected years of schooling.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: How is the 'income' component of HDI measured?
back: Real GNI (gross national income) per capita.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: How is longevity measured in the HDI?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What three indicators make up the Physical Quality of Life Index (PQLI)?
back: Literacy rate, infant mortality rate, and life expectancy at one year.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: Which life-expectancy measure does the PQLI use, in contrast to the HDI?
back: Life expectancy at one year (the HDI uses life expectancy at birth).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What three indicators make up the Global Hunger Index (GHI) as listed in the notes?
back: Proportion of the population that is undernourished, prevalence of underweight in children under five (%), and proportion of children dying before the age of five (%).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What does DALY stand for and how is it calculated?
back: Disability-Adjusted Life Years = YLD + YLL (years lived with disability + years of life lost).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: Is DALY a positive or negative outcome measure, and what does it measure?
back: A negative outcome; it measures disease burden.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: What does QALY stand for?
back: Quality-Adjusted Life Year - a year spent in perfect health.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: Is QALY a positive or negative outcome measure, and what does it measure?
back: A positive outcome; it measures cost-effectiveness of an intervention.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: Which index measures disease burden: DALY or QALY?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-1: Concept of Health, Natural History & Health Indices
front: Which index measures cost-effectiveness of an intervention: DALY or QALY?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance

back: Secular trend; example is NCDs (non-communicable diseases).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: Measles every 2-3 years; rubella every 6-7 years.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: What does primordial prevention target, and what is its main intervention?
back: It eliminates/prevents risk factors (xx R/F); main intervention is health education.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: What does primary prevention act on, and what are its two interventions?
back: It acts on risk factors to prevent disease (controls R/F, prevents disease); interventions are health promotion and specific protection.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: Give examples of specific protection (a primary-prevention intervention).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: What does secondary prevention aim to do, and through which interventions?
back: Reduce disease; through early diagnosis (screening) and treatment.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: What does tertiary prevention aim to do, and through which interventions?
back: Reduce disability; through disability limitation and rehabilitation.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance

back: Secondary (2 degree); examples are mammography, Pap smear, and PSA.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: Reducing disease burden; it is local in scope.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: What defines 'elimination' of a disease?
back: The chain of transmission is interrupted.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: What defines 'eradication' of a disease, and at what scope?
back: The organism itself is removed (zero); it is global in scope.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: What is passive surveillance?
back: The patient comes to the hospital (data flows in passively).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: What is active surveillance, and give examples of diseases using it.
back: Health workers go into the community to find patients; examples: malaria, TB, polio, and kala-azar.

back: The most common cause of epidemics.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: In the polio context, what is P3 associated with?
back: VAPP (vaccine-associated paralytic polio), due to an aberrant immune response.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: What was the OPV switch, when did it occur, and why?
back: tOPV was switched to bOPV on 25 April 2016, because P2 posed a high risk to the general population.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance

back: More than 1 per 1 lakh children under 15 years per year.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: AFP surveillance covers children of what age?
back: Under 15 years (<15 yrs) with acute flaccid paralysis.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: How many stool samples are collected in AFP surveillance and with what timing?
back: 2 stool samples, 24 hours apart, within 14 days of onset of paralysis.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: Biennial (once in 2 years); it is unlinked and anonymous.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-2: Disease Patterns, Levels of Prevention & Surveillance
front: What are the 'bridge groups' in HIV sentinel surveillance?
back: Single male migrants and truck drivers (carry infection from high-risk to general population).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: In a ratio, what is the relationship between numerator and denominator?
back: They are not linked - the numerator is not part of the denominator (no overlap).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: In a proportion, what is the relationship of numerator to denominator?
back: The numerator is a part of the denominator (expressed as a percentage).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: What distinguishes a rate from a ratio or proportion?
back: A rate includes an element of time.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: What is the formula for prevalence?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: What is the formula for incidence?
back: (New cases / population at risk) x 1000, per unit time.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: What is the formula for attack rate, and what is it used for?
back: (New cases / population at risk) x 100; used in infectious disease to show the extent of an epidemic/infection.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: What is the formula for secondary attack rate?
back: (Number of secondary cases within one incubation period / susceptible people) x 100.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: What is the formula for case fatality rate?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: What is the formula for proportional mortality rate?
back: (Number of deaths due to a cause / total number of deaths) x 100.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: Write the equation linking prevalence, incidence and duration.
back: P = I x D (Prevalence = Incidence x Duration).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: Increased survival has what effect on incidence and prevalence?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: Faster recovery has what effect on incidence and prevalence?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: Vaccination has what effect on incidence and prevalence?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: Primordial prevention has what effect on incidence and prevalence?

deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: Which measure indicates the virulence / killing power of a disease?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: Which measure indicates the communicability of a disease?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-3: Measures of Disease Frequency & Standardization
front: When is indirect standardization used?
back: When age-specific death rates of the reference population are not available.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the purpose of a descriptive study, and is it observational or experimental?
back: To formulate a hypothesis; it is observational.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the purpose of an analytical study, and is it observational or experimental?
back: To test the hypothesis; it is observational.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the purpose of an experimental study?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the reporting guideline for observational studies?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the reporting guideline for case reports?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the reporting guideline for RCTs?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: An ecological / correlation study is also called what, and what type of data does it use?
back: Aggregation study (population-based); it uses secondary (2 degree) data.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Ecological studies are prone to which fallacy?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: A cross-sectional study has what other names, and what data type does it use?
back: Prevalence study or snapshot study; it uses primary (1 degree) data.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: A case-control study has what other name, and what does it start with?
back: Case-reference study; it starts with disease.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: A cohort study has what other name, what does it start with, and what does it measure?
back: Follow-up study; it starts with exposure, follows for new cases, and measures incidence.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What are the individual-based descriptive study designs?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: List the experimental study designs given.
back: RCT, non-randomized studies, clinical trials, crossover trials, field trials, and community trials.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Which study design is best for studying multiple exposures?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Which study design is best for studying multiple outcomes?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Which study design is best for rare diseases?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Which study design is best for rare or expensive investigations?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Which design is an incidence study?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Which design is a prevalence / snapshot study?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the unit of study in an ecological study?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Intention-to-treat analysis is used in which design?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Bias is defined as what kind of error?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Selection bias is most associated with which study design?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Recall bias is most associated with which study design?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is Berksonian bias?
back: Bias from using hospital patients as the study group.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is attrition bias, and in which design does it occur?
back: Loss to follow-up; it occurs in cohort / follow-up studies.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is Hawthorne bias?
back: Subjects change their behaviour because they know they are being observed (the attention effect); seen in cohort studies.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is Pygmalion bias?
back: Bias arising from the researcher's belief/expectation.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Incidence-prevalence bias is also known by what other names?
back: Neyman bias / survival bias (it captures survivors).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: In a nested case-control study, how are cases and controls obtained?
back: From a defined cohort: those who develop the disease are cases, and a subgroup of those who do not develop it are selected as controls.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the best method to eliminate KNOWN confounders?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Which methods can address BOTH known and unknown confounders?
back: Randomization, restriction, stratification, stratified randomization, and multivariate analysis / statistical modelling.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the overall best method for handling confounders?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: In the smoking -> Ca lung example, what role does caffeine play?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: In the smoking -> Ca lung example, what role does asbestos play?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Matching deals with a confounder or an effect modifier?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: In single blinding, who is blinded?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: In double blinding, who is blinded, and how common is it?
back: The patient and the doctor; it is the most common type.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: In triple blinding, who is blinded, and how is it rated?
back: The patient, doctor, and data analyst; it is the best.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Blinding primarily removes which problem?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What does randomisation give subjects, and which problems does it remove?
back: A known and equal chance; it removes both bias and confounding.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: DTM Stage 2: BR, DR, growth?
back: High BR, rapidly falling DR, rapid increase.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: DTM Stage 3: BR, DR, growth?
back: Falling BR, slowly falling DR, increase slows.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: DTM Stage 4: BR, DR, pyramid?
back: Low BR, low DR, falling then stable; pyramid is spindle/urn shaped.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: Yet to be seen; BR possibly falling further, little change.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define total fertility rate (TFR).
back: Sum of age-specific fertility rates (ASFR) for all reproductive age groups for a particular period.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What does TFR equal, its target, and what is it best for?
back: Equals complete family size; target 2.1; best for assessing impact of family planning.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define gross reproduction rate (GRR).
back: Sum of ASFR for births of daughters across all reproductive age groups for a particular period.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define net reproduction rate (NRR).
back: Average number of daughters that would be borne by a newborn female, given specified mortality and ASFR.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: NRR is best for what, and its target?
back: Best measure of growth of population; target 1.0.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: GFR (general fertility rate) formula?
back: (Number of births to women aged 15-44/49)/(mid-year population of women aged 15-49) x 1000.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Maternal mortality ratio formula and definition of maternal death?
back: (Maternal deaths / live births) x 1 lakh; a pregnancy-related death within 42 days.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Maternal mortality rate formula?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: IMR formula?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Perinatal mortality ratio formula?
back: [Stillbirths >28 wk POG or >1000 g + early neonatal deaths (0-7 d)] / total births x 1000.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Rabies Category I exposures and PEP?
back: Touching/feeding animals, licks on intact skin; PEP = none.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Rabies Category II exposures and PEP?
back: Nibbling of uncovered skin, minor scratches/abrasions without bleeding; PEP = immediate vaccination + local wound treatment (no suturing).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Rabies Category III exposures and PEP?
back: Single/multiple transdermal bites or scratches (bleeding), licks on broken skin, mucous-membrane contamination with saliva, contact with bats; PEP = immediate vaccination + rabies immunoglobulin + local wound treatment.

back: Asymptomatic, or persistent generalized lymphadenopathy only.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-15: HIV/AIDS - Virology, NACP, Staging, OI Prophylaxis & Drugs
front: WHO stage 2 (mild disease) features?
back: Weight loss 5-10%, sores/cracks around lip, seborrhoea, prurigo, herpes zoster, recurrent URTI, recurrent mouth ulcers.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-15: HIV/AIDS - Virology, NACP, Staging, OI Prophylaxis & Drugs
front: WHO stage 3 (moderate disease) features?
back: Weight loss >10%, oral thrush, oral hairy leukoplakia, diarrhoea >1 month, unexplained fever, severe bacterial infection, pneumonia, pulmonary TB, TB lymphadenopathy, acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-15: HIV/AIDS - Virology, NACP, Staging, OI Prophylaxis & Drugs
front: WHO stage 4 (AIDS) features?
back: HIV wasting syndrome, oesophageal thrush, herpes simplex ulceration >1 month, lymphoma, Kaposi sarcoma, invasive cervical cancer, Pneumocystis pneumonia, extrapulmonary TB, cryptococcal meningitis, toxoplasma brain abscess, visceral leishmaniasis, HIV encephalopathy.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-15: HIV/AIDS - Virology, NACP, Staging, OI Prophylaxis & Drugs
front: AIDS-defining features?
back: >10% weight loss with >1 month diarrhoea and fever/weakness.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-15: HIV/AIDS - Virology, NACP, Staging, OI Prophylaxis & Drugs
front: When is first-line ART started relative to clinical stage/CD4?
back: Irrespective of clinical stage or CD4 count (any value).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
deck: Part-15: HIV/AIDS - Virology, NACP, Staging, OI Prophylaxis & Drugs
front: In HIV-TB co-infection, which is started first and when is ART added?
back: Start ATT first; start ART once TB treatment is tolerated (between 2 weeks and 2 months).

front: A couple has 4 unvaccinated children; one (index) gets measles on Aug 5 and two more by Aug 15. Secondary attack rate?
back: SAR = secondary cases / susceptibles = 2/(4-1) = 66% (computed; no answer marked in source).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Male diagnosed with oral cancer at 40; life expectancy 70; dies at 50; with intervention would survive to 60. DALY and QALY?
back: DALY = YLL + YLD = (70-50)+(50-40) = 20+10 = 30; QALY gained = 60-50 = 10 -> DALY 30, QALY 10 (computed; interpretation may vary; no answer marked).

Formulation of hypothesis; 7. Testing of hypothesis; 8. Evaluation of ecological factors; 9. Further investigation of population at risk; 10. Writing the report.

back: 0.10-0.20 slight, 0.21-0.40 fair, 0.41-0.60 moderate, 0.61-0.80 substantial, 0.81-0.99 near-perfect, 1 perfect agreement.

front: Regression formula types?
back: Simple linear (y=a+bx), multiple linear (y=a+b1x1+...+bnxn), curvilinear (y=a+bx+cx^2), and logarithmic = logistic regression.

front: What does the 'apples and oranges' problem refer to?
back: A drawback of meta-analysis (combining dissimilar studies).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What does the 'file drawer effect' refer to?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: List the modes of DIRECT transmission.
back: Droplet transmission, vertical, inoculation onto skin/mucosa, and contact with soil.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: List the modes of INDIRECT transmission.
back: Fomite-borne, airborne/droplet nuclei, food-borne, blood-borne, and vector-borne.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Airborne droplet nuclei: size, travel distance, and examples?
back: <5 microns, travel >1 m; examples TB, influenza, chickenpox, measles, COVID-19.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Vector transmission - propagative type and example?
back: Agent only multiplies; e.g., plague bacilli in rat fleas, yellow fever in Aedes.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Vector transmission - cyclo-propagative type and example?
back: Proliferation + development; e.g., malarial parasites in Anopheles.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Vector transmission - cyclo-developmental type and example?
back: Development only (no multiplication); e.g., microfilaria in Culex.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Vector transmission - transovarial type and example?
back: Passed via female eggs; e.g., Rickettsia rickettsii in ticks, mite in scrub typhus.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Vector transmission - transstadial type and example?
back: Passed across developmental stages (larva-nymph-adult); e.g., Borrelia burgdorferi and Indian tick typhus in ticks.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define anthropozoonoses with examples.
back: Disease transmitted from animals to humans; e.g., rabies, plague, hydatid.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define zooanthroponoses with example.
back: Disease transmitted from humans to animals; e.g., TB to cattle.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define amphixenosis with examples.
back: Disease maintained in both humans and animals; e.g., T. cruzi, S. japonicum.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define 'source' vs 'reservoir' of infection.
back: Source = person/animal/object from which the agent passes to the host; reservoir = where the organism metabolizes and replicates.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Hookworm: reservoir and source?
back: Reservoir = man; source = soil contaminated with infective larvae.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Tetanus: reservoir and source?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Typhoid: reservoir and source?
back: Reservoir = case or carrier; source = faeces or urine of patients.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define endemic, hyper-endemic, and holo-endemic.
back: Endemic = constant level; hyper-endemic = high levels in all age groups; holo-endemic = high in children.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: In which year was trachoma eliminated (per PDF 1)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: WHO drinking-water acceptability criteria?
back: Turbidity <=5 NTU, colour <=15 TCU, TDS <300 mg/L, pH 6.5-8.5, nitrates <45 mg/L; most undesirable metal is lead (Pb).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Rapid vs slow sand filter - space and sand size?
back: Rapid: very little space, sand 0.4-0.7 mm; slow: large area, sand 0.2-0.3 mm.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Rapid vs slow sand filter - mechanism and skill?
back: Rapid: chemical coagulation + sedimentation, highly skilled; slow: plain sedimentation, less skilled.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Rapid vs slow sand filter - loss of head and bacteria removal?
back: Rapid: 6-8 ft head loss, 98-99% removal; slow: ~4 ft head loss, 99.9-99.99% removal.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Methods of refuse disposal - worst and best?
back: Dumping is worst; controlled tipping/sanitary landfill is best (trench 2-3 m deep, 4-12 m wide; 1 acre per 10,000 population).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the Bangalore method of composting?
back: Anaerobic hot fermentation (integrated refuse + night-soil disposal).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define BOD.
back: Biological oxygen demand: oxygen required for aerobic destruction of organic matter; normal 100-300 mg/L.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define COD and how it is best estimated.
back: Chemical oxygen demand: oxygen equivalent needed for chemical oxidation of organic matter; best estimated by potassium dichromate.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Normal suspended solids in sewage?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Sewage treatment - primary and secondary steps?
back: Primary: screening, grit chamber, primary sedimentation. Secondary: trickling filter and aeration (most important step) - activated sludge.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Septic tank - where used and what happens inside vs outside?
back: Used where no STP exists; inside tank = anaerobic digestion, outside tank = aerobic oxidation.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Types of mechanical ventilation?
back: Exhaust (removes stale air), plenum (pushes fresh air in), balanced, and air-conditioning (filter + temperature control).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Definition of overcrowding (per notes)?
back: Two persons aged >9 years of opposite gender sharing the same room (except husband and wife).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Per-capita house space (PDF 1 value, resolving PDF 2 ambiguity)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Phases of the family life cycle?
back: Formation (marriage), extension (1st child born), complete extension (last child born), contraction (1st child leaves), complete contraction (last child leaves), dissolution (one spouse dies), extinction (both spouses die).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Family types: electron, nuclear, joint, 3-generation, new?
back: Electron = single-parent family; nuclear = husband+wife+children; joint = >=2 couples (males in blood relation); 3-generation = three generations; new family = <=10 years old.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: MC (most common) emotion of man (per notes)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define culture and acculturation.
back: Culture = learned behaviour that is socially acquired; acculturation = mixing of two cultures.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define folkways and mores.
back: Folkways = right ways of doing things according to places/cultures; mores = stringent customs.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Differentiate crowd, mob, and herd.
back: Crowd = people gathered temporarily by common interest/curiosity; mob = a leader forces members into action (temporary, emotional); herd = a leader whose orders the crowd follows without question.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Socioeconomic scales: urban vs rural?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Sequence of disaster impact phases?
back: Response - rehabilitation - reconstruction - development - prevention - mitigation - preparedness.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Which tool is used for injury prevention during road traffic accidents?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Who chairs disaster management (per notes)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: BMI is also called which index, and its formula?
back: Quetelet index (most commonly used); BMI = weight(kg)/[height(m)]^2.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Ponderal index formula?
back: Height(cm) / (cube root of body weight in kg).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Broca index for ideal weight?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Corpulence index formula and cut-off?
back: Actual weight / desirable weight; should not exceed 1.2 (most commonly used).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Skin-fold thickness: main site, sum cut-offs, instrument?
back: Mid-triceps (MC), also biceps/subscapular/suprailiac; sum <40 mm in males, <50 mm in females; measured by Harpenden calliper.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Dangerous (respirable) particle size?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Exceptions to the Hardy-Weinberg law?
back: Small/dynamic populations, non-random mating, mutations, gene outflow and genetic drift, and migration.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Health-care utilisation indicators?
back: Bed-turnover ratio, bed-occupancy rate, average length of stay, and % infants immunised.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Health-care delivery indices?
back: Doctor-population ratio, population-bed ratio, and population per health centre.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Immediately notifiable (within 24 hours) diseases?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: When and where was the WHO founded?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Locations of NIE and NIN?
back: National Institute of Epidemiology - Chennai; National Institute of Nutrition - Hyderabad.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What does the AMRIT scheme provide?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is DPDP 2023?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is emporiatrics?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Ottawa Charter strategies for health promotion?
back: Build healthy public policy, create supportive environments, strengthen community action, develop personal skills, and reorient health services.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Overall leading cancer (both sexes, per notes)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Kishori Shakti Yojana targets whom?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Rajiv Gandhi Shramik Kalyan Yojana provides what?
back: Unemployment allowance equal to 50% of wage for a maximum of 2 years.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is the 'web of causation'?
back: A model in which disease results from interacting chains of many causal factors.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Proportional mortality rate is described as which measure of burden (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: In chronic diseases, how do prevalence and incidence compare (coreBTR)?
back: P >> I (prevalence much greater than incidence).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: In acute diseases, how do prevalence and incidence compare (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Chickenpox/herpes zoster: incubation period and isolation duration (coreBTR)?
back: IP 10-21 days; isolate 6 days after onset of rash / until lesions crust.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Measles: incubation period and isolation duration (coreBTR)?
back: IP 8-14 days; isolate from onset of catarrhal stage to 5 days after rash onset.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Diphtheria: incubation, isolation, and communicable period (coreBTR)?
back: IP 2-5 days; isolate until 48 hours after starting antibiotics; communicable up to 4 weeks after disease onset.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Cholera: incubation period and isolation (coreBTR)?
back: IP 2 days; isolate until 3 days after starting tetracyclines.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Rubella cyclical periodicity - coreBTR value?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: PCV schedule per coreBTR?
back: PCV-1 at 6 weeks, PCV-2 at 14 weeks, PCV-booster at 9 months.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: fIPV schedule per coreBTR?
back: fIPV-1 at 6 weeks, fIPV-2 at 14 weeks, fIPV-3 at 9 months.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: OPV-0 (birth dose) is given within what time (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Vaccines at 9 months per coreBTR?
back: MR-1, Vitamin A, PCV-booster, fIPV-3, and JE-1.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Vaccines at 16-24 months per coreBTR?
back: MR-2, DPT 1st booster, OPV booster, and JE-2.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Define 'fully immunized' vs 'completely immunized' child (coreBTR)?
back: Fully immunized = immunized by 1 year; completely immunized = by 2 years.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Immunoglobulin should not be given together with vaccines, except which (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Heat-sensitivity order of vaccines (most to least, coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Freeze-sensitivity order of vaccines (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Cold chain storage durations (coreBTR)?
back: Small ILR/DF at PHC and large ILR/DF at district: 1 month; WIC/WIF at state/regional: 3 months.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: How often and by whom is the dial thermometer read (coreBTR)?
back: By the Medical Officer at the PHC, twice a day.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What is an absolute contraindication to vaccination (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Progressive neurological deficit after DPT - what should be given (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Is inconsolable cry / HHE after DPT a contraindication (coreBTR)?
back: No - it is not a contraindication.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: Within 5 days of exposure; OKA strain.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Yellow fever 17D - safety in pregnancy and protection (coreBTR)?
back: Safe in pregnancy; gives lifetime protection.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Dukoral cholera vaccine - composition and age (coreBTR)?
back: Cholera O1 + B subunit + bicarbonate buffer; given >2 years.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Pneumococcal subunit vaccines (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: TCV (typhoid conjugate vaccine) - age and dose (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Covaxin strain and minimum age (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Covishield minimum age (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Are deaths from accidents/electrocution/RTA counted as maternal deaths (coreBTR)?
back: No - they are excluded; a maternal death must occur within 42 days of delivery (intrapartum or up to 42 days postpartum).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Most common cause of maternal death (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Prudent diet (WHO Expert Committee) limits - fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, salt (coreBTR)?
back: Total fat 20-30% of energy; saturated fat <10% (ideal <7%); cholesterol <100 mg/1000 kcal/day; cholesterol:HDL <3.5; salt <=5 g/day.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Lifestyle SBP reductions (coreBTR)?
back: Weight reduction 5-20 mmHg per 10 kg; DASH plan 8-14 mmHg; sodium reduction 2-8 mmHg; physical activity 4-9 mmHg; alcohol moderation 2-4 mmHg.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Protein requirement (RDA, coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Nalgonda (NEERI) defluoridation technique uses what (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Limiting amino acids - cereal, maize, pulse (coreBTR)?
back: Cereal: threonine & lysine; maize: lysine & tryptophan; pulse: cysteine & methionine.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Aflatoxin is associated with which cancer (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: ICDS supplementary nutrition for severely malnourished children (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: ICDS protein ranges - 6m-6y and pregnant/lactating (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: AWC and CDPO norms (coreBTR)?
back: 1 AWC per 400-800 population; 1 CDPO per 100 AWW (1 block / 1 lakh population).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Mid Day Meal covers which school classes (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: National Sickle Cell Anaemia Elimination Mission - target year and management (coreBTR)?
back: By 2047; penicillin prophylaxis x 5 years; crisis management = hydration + hydroxyurea + analgesia.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: IFA tablet colours by age group (coreBTR)?
back: 6-59 months: pink (syrup); 5-9 years: blue; 10-19 years, 20-49 females, pregnant and lactating: red.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Growth chart yellow zone - meaning and management (coreBTR)?
back: Mild-to-moderate malnutrition (-2 to -3 SD); home-based management.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Surveillance levels - syndromic, presumptive, lab-confirmed (coreBTR)?
back: Syndromic = grassroots workers/MPW; presumptive = doctor/MO (clinical features + exam); lab-confirmed = laboratory positive.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Urban CHC population norm (coreBTR) - and the discrepancy?
back: coreBTR: 1 per 2.5 lakh (note: Cerebellum/PDF 2 stated 1 per 1.25 lakh).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: CHC specialist staff (coreBTR)?
back: Medicine, OBG, Surgery, Paediatrics, Ophthalmology, and Anaesthesia.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: PHC bed numbers - Type A vs Type B (coreBTR)?
back: Type A (<20 deliveries/month): 4-6 beds; Type B (>20/month): 6-10 beds.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Sub-centre bed numbers - Type A vs Type B (coreBTR)?
back: Type A: 1-2 beds; Type B: 2-3 beds.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: GATHER counselling steps (coreBTR)?
back: Greet, Ask, Tell, Help, Educate, Review.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Health education vs propaganda - key contrast (coreBTR)?
back: Health education: knowledge actively acquired, behaviour-centred, appeals to reason, develops individuality; propaganda: knowledge instilled passively, information-centred, appeals to emotion, develops a standard pattern.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Technique of interview - the 10 steps (coreBTR)?
back: Establishing contact, starting the interview, securing rapport, recall, probe questions, encouragement, guiding the interview, recording, closing the interview, report.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Adoption model stages as written in coreBTR?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: NITI Aayog - role and chair (coreBTR)?
back: Replaced the committees in 2015; develops the Health Index; chaired by the PM.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: NPCDCS conditions covered (coreBTR)?
back: Cancer, diabetes, CVD/stroke, COPD, NAFLD, CKD, and ARF (Jai Vigyan Mission).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Life expectancy at birth range used in HDI (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: A screening test should have high ___ and a diagnostic/confirmatory test high ___ (coreBTR)?
back: Screening = high sensitivity; diagnostic = high specificity.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: SnNout / SpPin rule (coreBTR)?
back: A highly Sensitive test, when Negative, rules OUT; a highly Specific test, when Positive, rules IN.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: Against the gold standard, using a Bland-Altman plot.

front: MCQ: new malaria test sensitivity 70%, specificity 90%; 200 smear-positive and 150 smear-negative tested - expected false negatives?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: ROC AUC values - ideal test, no predictive value, actual test (coreBTR)?
back: Ideal test AUC = 1; no predictive value AUC = 0.5; actual test 0.5 < AUC < 1.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Youden index formula and optimal cut-off (coreBTR)?
back: J = Sn + Sp - 1; the optimal cut-off is at maximum AUC.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: Log-rank test or Cox regression; the Hall-Wellner band.

back: NBCC (Newborn Care Corner) at PHC; NBSU (Newborn Stabilisation Unit) at FRU/CHC; SNCU (Special Newborn Care Unit) at the district hospital.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: 10-19 years; the clinic is the AFHC (Adolescent Friendly Health Clinic).

back: Rs.3000 at ANC registration; Rs.2000 at institutional delivery + all vaccines till 14 weeks; Rs.6000 if the 2nd child is a girl.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: Within 1 hour of a normal vaginal delivery; within 4 hours of an LSCS.

back: Personnel for newborn care and safer delivery.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: Single-digit NMR and stillbirth rate by 2030.

back: Loss of foot = impairment; cannot walk = disability; unemployed (as a result) = handicap.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: Temporality, strength of association, consistency, specificity, biological gradient (dose-response), biological plausibility, coherence, experiment, and analogy.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Hill's criteria - what is 'biological gradient' (coreBTR)?

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: The association does not contradict known facts of the disease.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Hill's criteria - what is 'experiment' (coreBTR)?
back: Removal or reduction of the exposure reduces disease occurrence.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

back: A study that is both prospective and retrospective.

back: De facto = counted as and where the person is; de jure = counted by jurisdiction (usual residence).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Vaccine wastage factors (coreBTR)?
back: BCG = 2; MR = 1.3; others = 1.1 (this is why 100 live births need 200 BCG doses).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Water-borne and water-based disease examples (coreBTR)?
back: Water-borne: Hep A/E and typhoid; water-based: Schistosoma and Dracunculus (guinea worm).

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: Monitoring vs evaluation - process/audit type (coreBTR)?
back: Monitoring = process measurement / internal audit; evaluation = outcome measurement / external audit.

subject: Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM)
front: What are ABC and VED analyses used for (coreBTR)?
back: ABC = Always Better Control (inventory/stock by value); VED = Vital, Essential, Desirable (inventory by criticality).
